questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The masses of neutron, proton and deutron in amu are 1.00893, 1.00813 and 2.01473, respectively. The packing fraction of the deutron in amu is
questions.solution:
$m_n=1.00893, m_p=1.00813, m_d=201473$
Deuteron $\left.{ }_1 \mathrm{H}^2\right)$ nucleus consist of one proton and one neutron.
$\begin{aligned}
\therefore \text {Mass defect } & \Delta m=\left(m_n+m_p\right)-m_d \\
& =(1.00893+1.00813)-201473 \\
& =0.00233
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \text {Packing fraction } & =\frac{\Delta m}{A} \\ & =\frac{0.00233}{2}=11.65 \times 10^{-4}\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Binding energy per nucleon versus mass number curve for nuclei is shown in the figure. W, X, Y and Z are four nuclei indicated on the curve. The process that would release energy is
questions.solution:
Energy is released in a process when total binding energy of the nucleus (= binding energy per nucleon x number of nucleons) is increased or we can say when total binding energy of products is more than the reactants.

In the reaction W→2Y

​​​​​​The binding energy of reactants = 120 x 7. 5 = 900 MeV
and binding energy of products = 2 (60 x 8. 5)=1020 MeV
and we can see 1020 MeV > 900 MeV.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A proton is bombarded on a stationary lithium nucleus. As a result of the collision two <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">α</mi></math>-particles are produced. If the direction of motion of the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">α</mi></math>-particles with the initial direction of motion makes an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac bevelled="true"><mn>1</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math>, then the kinetic energy of the striking proton is [Given, binding energies per nucleon of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Li</mi><mn>7</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math>  and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>He</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi>proton</mi></msub><mo>≈</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi>neutron</mi></msub></math>]
questions.solution:
Q value of the reaction is,

                                 Q = (2 × 4 × 7.06 – 7 × 5.6) MeV = 17.28 MeV



Applying conservation of energy for collision,

                                 Kp + Q = 2 Kα                 ....(i)

(Here, Kp and Kα are the kinetic energies of proton and α - particle respectively)

From the conservation of linear momentum (As there is no external force) ....(ii)

                               [Here P=2mk]
                                 ⇒ K⁡ p⁡ = 1 6 K⁡ α cos 2 θ = 1 6 K⁡ α 1 4 2 as m⁡ α = 4 m⁡ p⁡

∴                                 Kα =Kp                 ....(iii)

Solving eqs. (i) and (iii) with Q = 17.28 MeV


we get               Kp = 17.28 MeV
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Assuming that about <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>MeV</mi></math> of energy is released per fusion reaction<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msub><mtext> </mtext><mn>1</mn></msub><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>→</mo><msub><mtext> </mtext><mn>0</mn></msub><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mn>1</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msub><mtext> </mtext><mn>2</mn></msub><msup><mi>He</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mo>,</mo></math> then the mass of<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> consumed per day in a fusion reactor of power <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>MW</mi></math> will approximately be
questions.solution:
Energy produced U=Pt

=106×24×36×102

=24×36×108 J

The energy released per fusion reaction

=20 MeV

=20×106×1.6×10-19

=32×10-13 J

The energy released per atom of 1H2

=32×10-13 J

Number of 1H2 atoms used =24×36×10832×10-13

=27×1021

Mass of 6×1023 atom =2 g

∴ Mass of 27×1021 atoms

=26×1023×27×1021

≈0.1g
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Consider the nuclear reaction <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mn>200</mn></msup><mo>→</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mn>110</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mn>80</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mn>1</mn></msup></math>. If the binding energy per nucleon for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math> respectively, then the energy released in the reaction -
questions.solution:
For X: energy E1=200×7.4=1480MeV

For A: energy E2=110×8.2=902MeV

For B: energy E3=80×8.1=648MeV

Energy Released E=E1+E2+E3

E=902+648-1480=70MeV
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: In <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> decay the kinetic energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> particle is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>48</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> value of the reaction is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>MeV</mi></math>. The mass number of the mother nucleus is (Assume that the daughter nucleus is in the ground state)
questions.solution:
Let Kα is kinetic energy of α particle and Kd is the kinetic energy of the daughter nucleus.

Let A be the mass number of mother nucleus then the mass number of the daughter nucleus is A-4.

From the conservation of momentum

KαKd=p2242A-4p2

KαKd=A-44

Kd=4A-4Kα

Q  value =Kα+Kd

50=48+4A-448

2=4A-448

A-4=96

A=100
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A heavy nucleus at rest breaks into two fragments which fly off with velocities in the ratio $3: 1$. The ratio of radii of the fragments is
questions.solution:
From law of conservation of momentum
$\begin{aligned} m v=\text { constant } & \\ V \rho v & =k \\ \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 \rho v & =k\end{aligned}$
$\left(\frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)^3=\frac{v_2}{v_1} \Rightarrow \frac{R_1}{R_2}=\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right)^{1 / 3}=\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{1 / 3}=1: 3^{1 / 3}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Choose the correct statement of the following
options: The nuclear density in general, is independent of mass number $A$.
The radius of nucleus is directly proportional to the mass number $A$ of the nucleus.
The binding energy of a nucleus is inversely proportional to its mass defect.
Energy is observed when heavy nuclei undergo. transmutation into light nuclei.
questions.solution:
We know that, the radius $(R)$ of nucleus is given as
$R=R_0 A^{1 / 3}$ or $R \propto A^{1 / 3}$
Where,
$\begin{aligned} R_0 & =\text { constant } \\ A & =\text { mass number }\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Nuclear density, $\rho=\frac{\text { mass }}{\text { volume }}=\frac{m A}{\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3}$ (here, $m$ is mass of each nucleon)
$\rho=\frac{3 m}{4 \pi R_0^3}$
Hence, it is clear from above formula, nuclear density is independent of mass number $A$. 
The binding energy, $E=\Delta m c^2$
i. e $E \propto \Delta m$
Where, $\Delta m=$ mass defect
Energy is released when heavy nuclei undergo transmutation into light nuclei.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Two nuclei have their mass numbers in the ratio of $1: 3$. The ratio of their nuclear densities would be
questions.solution:
Density of nuclear matter is independent of mass number, so the required ratio is $1: 1$. 
Alternative
$$
A_{1}: A_{2}=1: 3
$$
Their radii will be in the ratio
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{0} A_{1}^{1 / 3}: R_{0} A_{2}^{1 / 3}=1: 3^{1 / 3} \\
\text { Density }=\frac{A}{\frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}} \\
\therefore \rho_{A_{1}}: \rho_{A_{2}}=\frac{1}{\frac{4}{3} \pi R_{0}^{3} \cdot 1^{3}}=\frac{3}{\frac{4}{3} \pi R_{0}^{3}\left(3^{1 / 3}\right)^{3}}
\end{aligned}
$$
Their nuclear densities will be the same.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
A shell of mass 'M' initially at rest suddenly explodes in three fragments. Two of
these fragments are of mass 'M $/ 4$ ' each, which move with velocities $3 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ and
$4 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ respectively in mutually perpendicular directions. The magnitude of velocity of the third fragment is
questions.solution:
The mass $M$ is initially at rest, hence its initial momentum is zero. By law or conservation of momentum, the net momentum of the three pieces should be zero. The moments of the two pieces are
$\mathrm{p}_{1}=\frac{3 \mathrm{M}}{4} \text { and } \mathrm{p}_{2}=\frac{5 \mathrm{M}}{4}$
Three are at right angles to each other.
Their resultant will be $p=\sqrt{p_{1}^{2}+p_{2}^{2}}$
$\begin{array}{l}
=\sqrt{\left(\frac{3 M}{4}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{4 M}{4}\right)^{2}} \\
=\frac{M}{4} \sqrt{(3)^{2}+(4)^{2}} \\
=\frac{M}{4} \sqrt{25}=\frac{5 M}{4}
\end{array}$
The momentum of the third piece will be equal and opposite to this.
The mass of the third piece will be $\frac{M}{2}$
If its velocity is $\mathrm{V}$, then
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{M}{2} \cdot V=\frac{5 M}{4} \\
\therefore V=2.5 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}
\end{array}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The radius of ${ }_{72} \mathrm{Te}^{125}$ nucleus is 6 fermi. The radius of ${ }_{13} \mathrm{Al}^{27}$ nucleus in meters is
questions.solution:
The relation between radius $(R)$ and atomic number $(A)$ is
$\frac{R_1}{R_2}=\left(\frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^{1 / 3}$
Given, $R_1=6$ fermi, $A_1=125, A_2=27$
$\begin{aligned}
\frac{6}{R_2} & =\left(\frac{125}{27}\right)^{1 / 3}=\frac{5}{3} \\
\Rightarrow R_2 & =\frac{6 \times 3}{5}=\frac{18}{5}=3.6 \text { fermi } \\
& =3.6 \times 10^{-15} \mathrm{~m}
\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If the mass defect of <math> <mrow> <msub> <mrow></mrow> <mn>5</mn> </msub> <msup> <mtext>B</mtext> <mrow> <mn>11</mn></mrow> </msup> </mrow> </math> is <math> <mrow> <mn>0.081</mn><mtext> </mtext><mtext>u</mtext></mrow></math>, its average binding energy (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>MeV</mi></math>) is
questions.solution:
Given,  Δ m for 5 B 1 1 = 0.0821 u

Number of nucleons = 11

​Binding energy =( 931×Δm )MeV

    = 9 3 1 × 0.081

    = 75.411 MeV

Average binding energy

   = Binding energy Number of nucleons

   = 75.411 11

   = 6.85 MeV
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>24.8</mtext><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> X-rays strike a material, the photoelectrons emitted from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> shell are observed to move in a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>23</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> in a magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The binding energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> shell electrons is
questions.solution:
evB=mv2R

⇒v=emBR

Kinetic energy of photoelectrons

K=12mv2=e2B2R22m

K=2.97×10-15J=18.6 KeV

K=EP-EK

EK=EP-K=24.8-18.6=6.2 KeV
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The graph of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ln</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mi>R</mi><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></mfrac></mfenced></math> versus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ln</mi><mi>A</mi></math> is where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is radius of a nucleus, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is its mass number, and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is constant
questions.solution:
Using the relation mass number A and radius R of a nucleus,

R=R0A13  ...(1)

Taking the logarithm of both sides,

lnR=lnR0A13=lnR0+13lnA⇒lnRR0=13lnA

Hence, the graph of lnRR0 vs. 13lnA will be a straight line.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If the binding energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>N</mi><mn>14</mn></msup></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math> per nucleon and that of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>N</mi><mn>15</mn></msup></math> is<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math> per nucleon. then the energy required to remove a neutron from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>N</mi><mn>15</mn></msup></math> is
questions.solution:
The total binding energy,

N14=7.5×14 MeV

N15=7.7×15 MeV

The energy required to remove a neutron from N15,

E=BE N15-BE N14

E=7.7×15-7.5×14 MeV

⇒  E=10.5 MeV
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Assume that a neutron breaks into a proton and electron. The energy released during this process is (Mass of neutron <math><mo>=</mo><mn>1.6725</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1.6725</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> , mass of electron <math><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>31</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> ) ,
questions.solution:
∆m=mp+me-mn

Energy released =∆m×c2
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
The mass defect in a particular nuclear reaction is $0.3 \mathrm{~g}$. The amount of energy liberated in kilowatt hour is :
(Velocity of light $=3 \times 10^8$ )
questions.solution:
$\Delta m=0.3 \mathrm{~g}$
$=0.3 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kg}=3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~kg}$
Energy liberated, $E=\Delta m c^2$
$=3 \times 10^{-4} \times\left(3 \times 10^8\right)^2$
$=3 \times 10^{-4} \times 9 \times 10^{16}$
$=27 \times 10^{12} \mathrm{~J}$
$=\frac{27 \times 10^{12}}{3.6 \times 10^6} \mathrm{kWh}$
$=7.5 \times 10^6 \mathrm{kWh}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If $200 \mathrm{MeV}$ of energy is released in the fission of one nucleus of ${ }_{92}^{236} \mathrm{U}$, the number of nuclei that must undergo fission to release an energy of $1000 \mathrm{~J}$ is
questions.solution:
Energy released in the fission of one nucleus
$\begin{aligned}
& =200 \mathrm{MeV} \\
& =200 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-13} \mathrm{~J} \\
& =3.2 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~J}
\end{aligned}$
Energy to be released
$=1000 \mathrm{~J}$
$3.2 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~J}$ energy is released due to number of nucleus fissioned $=1$
$\therefore 1000 \mathrm{~J}$ energy is released due to number of nucleus fissioned
$\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{1 \times 1000}{3.2 \times 10^{-11}} \\
& =3.125 \times 10^{13} \text { nucleus }
\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
A neutron is absorbed by a   3 6 Li  nucleus with the subsequent emission of an alpha particle.

 L36i+n01→H24e+H13+Q

Calculate the energy released, in MeV, in this reaction.

[Given: mass  36Li=6.015126 u;   mass (neutron) =1.0086654 u;

Mass (alpha particle) =4.0026044 u and

Mass (tritium) =3.0100000 u.

[ Take  1 u=931 MeV c-2 ]
questions.solution:
L36i+n24→H24e+H13+Q

Total initial mass =6.015126+1.0086654

                               =7.0237914 amu

  Total final mass =4.0026044+3.01

                               =7.0126044 amu

Mass defect,   Δm=7.0237914−7.0126044

                               =0.0111870 amu

Energy released, Q=0.0111870×931

                                 =10.415 MeV.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A moving positron and electron both with kinetic energy<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math> annihilate with each other and emits two gamma photons. If the rest mass energy of an electron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>51</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math>, the wavelength of each photon is ?
questions.solution:
Energy released in annihilation =0.51+0.51=1.02 MeV

Initial energy =1+1=2 MeV .

Therefore, the energy of the two photons is =1.02+2=3.02 MeV . Hence energy of each photon is E=1.51 MeV . Now, according to Duane-Hunt law, the wavelength of a photon of energy E(in eV) is given by

λ=hcEineV Å=6.62×10−34×3×1081.51×106 Å

=8.2×10−3 Å
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: $\mathrm{AU}^{235}$ nuclear reactor generates energy at a rate of $3.70 \times 10^7 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{s}$. Each fission liberates $185 \mathrm{MeV}$ useful energy. If the reactor has to operate for $144 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~s}$, then, the mass of the fuel needed is (Assume Avogadro's number $\left.=6 \times 10^{23} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}, 1 \mathrm{eV}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}\right)$
questions.solution:
In $1 \mathrm{~s}$, energy generated is $3.7 \times 10^7 \mathrm{~J}$
In $144 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~s}$, energy generated is
$=3.7 \times 10^7 \times 144 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~J}$
Also energy released in one fission is
$\begin{aligned}
& =185 \mathrm{meV} \\
& =185 \times 10^6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}
\end{aligned}$
Number of fission $=\frac{3.7 \times 10^7 \times 144 \times 10^4}{185 \times 10^6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}$
$=1.8 \times 10^{24} \text { of } \mathrm{U}^{235} \text { atoms. }$
Mass contained in $1.8 \times 10^{24}$ atoms of $U^{235}$ is
$=\frac{235 \times 1.8 \times 10^{24}}{6.023 \times 10^{23}}=702.3 \mathrm{~g}=0.70 \mathrm{~kg}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If the radius of a nucleus with mass number 125 is 1.5 fermi, then radius of a nucleus with mass number 64 is
questions.solution:
By Rutherford's experiment
$\begin{aligned}
R_1 & =R_0 A_1^{1 / 3} \\
R_2 & =R_0 A_2^{1 / 3}
\end{aligned}$
Given
$\begin{aligned}
R_1 & =1.5 \text { fermi } \\
A_1 & =125 \\
A_2 & =64 \\
R_2 & =?
\end{aligned}$
Now, $\frac{R_1}{R_2}=\left(\frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^{1 / 3} \quad$ [from the Eqs. (i) and (ii)]
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1.5}{R_2}=\left(\frac{125}{64}\right)^{1 / 3} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1.5}{R_2}=\frac{5}{4} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad R_2=\frac{1.5 \times 4}{5}=0.3 \times 4
\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
A star initially has 1040 deuterons. It produces energy via the processes 1 H 2 + 1 H 2 ⟶ 1 H 3 + p  &  1 H 2 + 1 H 3 ⟶ 2 He 4 + n . If the average power radiated by the star is 1016 W, the deuteron supply of the star is exhausted in a time of the order of

(mass of H21=2.014 amu, mass of He42=4.001 amu, mp=1.007 amu, mn=1.008 amu)
options: 106 s
108 s
1012 s
1016 s
questions.solution:
The given reactions are:

1H2 + 1H2 → 1H3 + p

1H2 + 1H3 → 1He4 + n

31H2→ 2He4 +n + p

Mass defect

Δm = (3 × 2. 014 - 4. 001 - 1. 007 - 1. 008) amu
= 0.026 amu

Energy released = 0. 026 × 931 MeV

= 0. 026 × 931 × 1. 6 × 10-12J = 3. 87 × 10-13 J

This is the energy produced by the consumption of three deuteron atoms.

Therefore Total energy released by 1040 deuterons

= 1 0 4 0 3 × 3. 87 × 10-12 J = 1. 29 × 1028 J

The average power radiated is P=1016 W or 1016 J s. Therefore, the total time to exhaust all deuterons of the star, time taken will be

t=1.29×10281016=1.29×1012 ≈ 1012
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The correct statement of the following is
options: The $Q$-value of a nuclear process is the difference between final and initial kinetic energies.
The nuclear mass is always higher than the total mass of its constituents.
Nuclides with same number of neutrons in the nucleus are known as isotopes.
In nuclear fusion, a heavy nucleus breaks into two smaller fragments.
questions.solution:
Option (b) is incorrect as, nuclear mass is lower than mass of constituents due to mass defect.
Option (c) is incorrect as, Nuclides with same number of protons are isotopes.
option (d) is incorrect as, In fusion two or more lighter nuclei combines to form a heavy nucleus.
Option (a) is correct; as in a nuclear reaction $Q$
value $=K \cdot E_{\text {final }}-K \cdot E_{\text {initial }}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If $200 \mathrm{MeV}$ of energy is released in the fission of one nucleus of ${ }_{92}^{235} \mathrm{U}$, then the number of nuclei that must undergo fission to release an energy of $1000 \mathrm{~J}$ is
questions.solution: $\begin{array}{rlrl}\text { Number of nuclei } & =\frac{E_{\text {total }}}{E_1}=\frac{1000 \mathrm{~J}}{200 \mathrm{MeV}} \\ & =\frac{1000 \mathrm{~J}}{200 \times 10^6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & N & =\frac{10^{14}}{3.2} \\ \Rightarrow & N & =3.125 \times 10^{13}\end{array}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: An <math> <mrow> <mi>α</mi> </mrow> </math>-particle of energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math> is scattered through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo>°</mo></math> by a fixed uranium nucleus. The distance of the closest approach is of the order of
questions.solution:
Let, at the distance of closest approach r0 from the nucleus, the α-particle stops a moment and gets scattered by the angle 180°.

Now charge on an α-particle q1=+2e and charge on a scattering nucleus q2=+Ze where Z=92 atomic number of uranium. 
The initial kinetic energy of the α-particle gets converted into electrostatic potential energy, Kα=U.
Kα=12mv2=kq1q2r0   .....(1), (k=9×109 N m2 C-2) 

Given- Kα=5 MeV=5×1.6×10-13 J
From the equation (1), we get,

⇒r0=k2eZeKα
⇒r0=2×9×109×92×1.6×10-1925×1.6×10-13
⇒r0=2649.65×10-16 m
r0=5.3×10-14 m =5.3×10-12 cm.

Therefore, the distance of the closest approach is in the order of, r0≈10-12 cm.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If an electron and a positron annihiliate, then the energy released is
questions.solution:
The amount of energy E produced by annihilation is

E=Mc2 (as per Einstein's equation)

Now, the mass of electron and positron is equal.

me-=me+=9.1×10-31 kg

Then, the energy released E

 =Mc2

=me-+me+c2

=2me-c2

=2×9.1×10-313×1082

=1.6×10-13 J
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Assume that the nuclear binding energy per nucleon (BA) versus mass number (A) is as shown in the figure. Use this plot to choose the correct choice given below.
options: Fusion of two nuclei with mass numbers lying in the range of 1 < A < 50 will release energy.
Fusion of two nuclei with mass numbers lying in the range of 51 < A < 100 will consume energy.
Fission of a nucleus lying in the mass range of 100 < A < 200 will release energy when broken into two equal fragments.
Fission of a nucleus lying in the mass range of 200 < A < 260 will release energy when broken into two equal fragments.
questions.solution:
In fusion, two or more lighter nuclei combine to make a comparatively heavier nucleus.
In fission, a heavy nucleus breaks into more than one comparatively lighter nucleus.
Further, energy will be released in a nuclear process if total binding energy increases.
Therefore Correct option is that the fission of a nucleus lying in the mass range of 200 < A < 260 will release energy when broken into two equal fragments.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If $200 \mathrm{MeV}$ of energy is released in the fission of one nucleus of ${ }_{92}^{236} \mathrm{U}$, the number of nuclei that must undergo fission to release an energy of $1000 \mathrm{~J}$ is
questions.solution:
Energy released in the fission of one nucleus
$\begin{aligned}
& =200 \mathrm{MeV} \\
& =200 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-13} \mathrm{~J} \\
& =3.2 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~J}
\end{aligned}$
Energy to be released
$=1000 \mathrm{~J}$
$3.2 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~J}$ energy is released due to number of nucleus fissioned $=1$
$\therefore 1000 \mathrm{~J}$ energy is released due to number of nucleus fissioned
$\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{1 \times 1000}{3.2 \times 10^{-11}} \\
& =3.125 \times 10^{13} \text { nucleus }
\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The binding energy per nucleon of deuteron (<math><mmultiscripts><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow><mprescripts></mprescripts><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mmultiscripts></math>and helium nucleus (<math><mmultiscripts><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow><mprescripts></mprescripts><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mmultiscripts></math>is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math>, respectively. If two deuteron nuclei react to form a single helium nucleus , then the energy released is
questions.solution:
As given,

 1H2 + 1H2→ 2He4 +energy

The binding energy per nucleon of a deuteron (1H2) =1.1 MeV

∴ Total binding energy of one deuteron nucleus = 2 × 1.1=2.2 MeV

∴ The binding energy per nucleon of Helium (2He4) =7 MeV

∴ Total binding energy = 4 × 7 =28 MeV

Hence, energy released in the above process =28-2 ×2.2

= 28 – 4.4 = 23.6 MeV
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
